g.data-narration: Welcome to exploring how compiler technology powers modern computing beyond traditional compilation.
g.text: The Ecosystem of Compiler Technology Applications
From Language Implementation to Hardware Design

g.data-narration: Compiler technology processes structured text using lexical analysis, parsing, semantic analysis, and code generation techniques.
The core value lies in processing structured text, analyzing program meaning, transforming code, and generating efficient machine output.
Compiler technology enables compilers (C, Rust), interpreters (Python), and JIT compilers (Java JVM, JavaScript V8) for language execution.
JIT compilation blends interpretation and AOT compilation, identifying hot code paths for runtime optimization.
Compilers optimize for modern processors through processor-specific code generation, automatic parallelization, and cache optimization.
Compilers and hardware design have a symbiotic relationship; compilers make new hardware features usable and influence architecture decisions.
Program translation includes binary translators like Rosetta 2, hardware synthesis from HDLs, and database query optimization.
Compiler phases power development tools: lexical analysis for highlighting, parsing for IDEs, semantic analysis for static analysis.
g.text: Examples: ESLint, Pylint, Prettier, VS Code, IntelliJ

g.data-narration: Compiler techniques enable automatic code generation for data marshaling in RPC and REST APIs from interface definitions.
Core compiler concepts in action: lexing powers tools, parsing enables IDEs, semantic analysis supports bug detection, and IR enables retargeting.
g.text: Intermediate Representation (IR) enables compiler retargeting for different CPUs

g.data-narration: AOT compilation offers predictable performance ahead-of-time, while JIT provides runtime optimization at the cost of startup overhead.
g.text: Fast startup • Predictable performance • C, Rust
Runtime optimization • Slower startup • Java, JS
Balance: Startup Time vs Peak Performance

g.data-narration: Key takeaways: think beyond compilation, link compiler phases to real-world tools, and use modern examples like Rosetta 2 and V8.
Compiler technology underpins modern computing from language implementations to hardware synthesis, making it essential for every software engineer.
